title: Listening - Part 4: Volunteering Abroad
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their experiences volunteering in different countries

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been working in finance for a decade, and [21]I felt a desperate need to do something that had a direct, tangible impact on people's lives[/21]. I volunteered to help build a school in a remote village in Nepal. It was physically punishing, but [26]the most profound outcome was the total shift in my perspective on what constitutes a 'necessity'[/26]. I came back and realized I didn't need half the stuff I owned. It’s made me much more mindful of my consumption and far more appreciative of the basic comforts I used to take for granted, like clean running water. || Speaker 2: I’m a retired nurse, and [22]I chose to volunteer at a wildlife sanctuary in Costa Rica because I’ve always been passionate about animal welfare and wanted to use my medical skills in a new context[/22]. It was a dream come true. However, [27]the biggest challenge was the extreme humidity, which left me feeling constantly exhausted and drained[/27]. I hadn't prepared for how much the climate would affect my ability to work long hours. I had to learn to pace myself, which was a tough lesson for someone used to the fast-paced environment of a busy hospital ward. || Speaker 3: I was looking for a way to improve my Spanish, so [23]I volunteered at a community center in Peru primarily because I wanted to immerse myself in the language and culture[/23]. I thought I’d be teaching English, but I ended up helping with local agricultural projects. [28]What I found most rewarding was the deep and lasting friendships I formed with the local families[/28]. They welcomed me into their homes and treated me like one of their own. It’s given me a sense of global connection that I never would have found as a typical tourist staying in hotels. || Speaker 4: I’m a student, and [24]I volunteered on a marine conservation project in Madagascar because I wanted to gain some practical experience for my biology degree[/24]. I wanted to see if field research was really for me. It was fascinating, but [29]the most difficult aspect was the absolute lack of any digital connection or internet for weeks at a time[/29]. I didn't realize how much I relied on my phone for a sense of security and entertainment. It was a forced digital detox that was incredibly uncomfortable at first, though eventually, I started to enjoy the mental stillness it provided. || Speaker 5: I recently went through a difficult divorce and [25]I volunteered at an orphanage in India because I needed to get away from my own problems and find a sense of purpose again[/25]. It was an emotional rollercoaster. [30]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive boost in my own emotional resilience[/30]. Seeing the strength and joy of those children, despite their difficult circumstances, made me realize that I was much stronger than I thought. I came home feeling empowered and ready to start the next chapter of my life with a much more positive and courageous outlook.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to volunteer.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to have a direct and tangible impact on people's lives.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to use her medical skills in the context of animal welfare.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to immerse himself in the language and culture of Peru.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to gain practical experience for her biology degree.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 needed a sense of purpose and to get away from personal problems after a divorce.

options.A: to fulfill a university graduation requirement
options.B: to test their suitability for a specific career
options.C: to achieve a sense of practical contribution
options.D: to escape a high-pressure professional role
options.E: to honor a promise made to a friend
options.F: to find a distraction from personal difficulties
options.G: to apply their existing professional expertise
options.H: to facilitate their learning of a foreign tongue

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a shift in perspective on what constitutes a 'necessity'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the extreme humidity and how it left her feeling exhausted.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the deep and lasting friendships he formed with local families.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the total lack of digital connection or internet very difficult.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 mentions a massive boost in her own emotional resilience.

options.A: the frustration of being digitally isolated
options.B: a newfound sense of personal strength
options.C: the realization that they were under-skilled
options.D: a change in their understanding of basic needs
options.E: the difficulty of communicating with locals
options.F: the physical impact of the local climate
options.G: a sense of regret about the time spent
options.H: the comfort of a new social network
